questions.question: What gap do MCP Apps (SEP-1724) close?
options: MCP servers could not return errors before
Tool output was text-only — interactive UI required client-specific widget APIs or nothing; Apps standardize sandboxed inline HTML across hosts
Servers could not run JavaScript
Models could not see images
questions.explanation: Before SEP-1724 (official January 26, 2026), a visualize_timeline tool returned a paragraph. With Apps, one HTML bundle behind a ui:// URI renders the same interactive timeline in Claude Desktop, ChatGPT, Cursor, Goose, and VS Code.

questions.question: How does a tool result reference its interactive UI?
options: Inline HTML directly in the text block
A ui:// resource URI in the content plus _meta.ui declaring resourceUri, CSP, and permissions; the host reads it with resources/read
A link the user must open in their browser
A base64-encoded video
questions.explanation: The result carries a ui_resource pointing at e.g. ui://notes/timeline; the host fetches it via resources/read and receives HTML with MIME text/html;profile=mcp-app, rendered per the declared CSP.

questions.question: How is the App's HTML contained by the host?
options: It runs in the host's main window with full DOM access
In a sandboxed iframe with server-declared CSP, no host cookies or localStorage, and network limited to connectSrc
In a separate OS process with root privileges
It is converted to static text before display
questions.explanation: The host renders the bundle in a sandboxed iframe (allow-scripts allow-same-origin or stricter), applies the declared CSP, and grants no host-origin storage — the UI gets exactly the network surface the CSP allows.

questions.question: Why must postMessage between the App iframe and host never use "*" as the target origin?
options: "*" is a syntax error in modern browsers
The channel carries tool calls and resource reads — without pinned origins and origin validation, any frame could inject or intercept them
It makes messages slower
The MCP spec charges per origin
questions.explanation: The iframe-host channel is a JSON-RPC dialect carrying privileged operations like host.callTool. Both sides must pin targetOrigin to the exact peer and validate event.origin against an allowlist before processing anything.

questions.question: What can the UI inside the iframe do via the postMessage JSON-RPC?
options: Nothing — it is display-only
Call host methods like host.callTool to invoke the server's tools (e.g. notes_update from an edit form), within granted permissions
Read any file on the user's machine
Replace the host's system prompt
questions.explanation: The dialect lets interactive UIs round-trip into the same MCP tool surface — an edit form posting host.callTool with notes_update — making the UI a first-class client of the server, gated by the declared permissions.
